title: Effectiveness of school-based suicide prevention interventions: An overview of systematic reviews
text:
Effectiveness of School-Based Suicide Prevention Interventions: An Overview of Systematic Reviews with Meta-Analyses on Suicide-Related Outcomes
Lisa Chinn1, Shaina D. Trevino1, Katarzyna Steinka-Fry1, Elizabeth Day1, Emily E. Tanner-Smith1, and Sean Grant1
1HEDCO Institute for Evidence-Based Educational Practice, College of Education, University of Oregon

crumbs: 1  Effectiveness of school-based suicide prevention interventions: An overview of systematic reviews with meta-analyses on suicide-related outcomes

title: Effectiveness of school-based suicide prevention interventions: An overview of systematic reviews
text: Author Note
crumbs: 1  Effectiveness of school-based suicide prevention interventions: An overview of systematic reviews with meta-analyses on suicide-related outcomes

title: 2  Overview
text:
1 Abstract
The objective of this overview is to examine the XX at primary and secondary schools in the United States. Compared to traditional systematic reviews estimating the effects of interventions, overviews explore XX, XX, and XX (CITATION?). Overviews are particularly useful for XX. Consequently, an overview can provide useful insights about XX for XX.
Background: Suicide is a leading cause of death in youth. This overview aims to summarize systematic reviews with meta-analyses estimating the effects of school-based suicide prevention interventions on suicide-related outcomes. Methods: We conducted electronic bibliographic searches, hand-searched relevant journals, and conducted forward and backward citation chasing to identify eligible reviews. Two reviewers independently screened records, assessed full texts for eligibility, and collected data. We quantified the overlap of primary studies across systematic reviews using Corrected Covered Area (CCA) and narratively summarized meta-analytic findings. Results: We identified 5 eligible systematic reviews with 78 primary studies overall. Only 36 primary studies (46.2%) were included in more than one review (CCA = 19.9%). We rated most reviews as critically low (80%) quality on AMSTAR-2 and high risk of bias (60%) on ROBIS. Reviews mostly suggest school-based suicide prevention interventions may have modest average positive impacts on suicidal behaviors and suicidal ideation, though mixed results on suicide-related outcomes by intervention approach, school level, and follow-up timing. Conclusions: School-based suicide prevention interventions may be beneficial on average, though existing reviews have important methodological limitations. A living systematic review conducted according to methodological best-practice could provide timely, relevant, and rigorous evidence for educators.

title: 2  Overview
text: *Keywords:* suicide, meta-analysis, school, systematic review, prevention, overview of reviews, umbrella review

title: 3  Background
text:
Suicide is a worldwide leading cause of death in youth (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2023; Curtin & Garnett, 2023; World Health Organization, 2025). Reductions in suicide could save numerous lives, and schools are a prevention setting that may be able to reach many young people (Ayer & Colpe, 2023). As interest-holders seek information on school-based suicide prevention, the existing research can be challenging to sift through, with extensive variation in study context and quality. This overview of systematic reviews synthesizes information from systematic reviews with meta-analysis on primary and secondary school-based suicide prevention. It also addresses issues with quality and risk of bias in suicide prevention systematic reviews.
Suicide rates are high in young people. In 2021, suicide was the fourth leading cause of death globally in ages 15-19 (World Health Organization, 2025). In 2023, suicide was the second most common cause of death for ages 10-24 in the U.S. (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2023). Research suggests that around 22 percent of U.S. middle schoolers surveyed in 2019 reported experiencing suicidal thoughts and 9 percent reported an attempt (Young et al., 2024). In some places, the problem has worsened over time. Between 2007 and 2018, U.S. suicide rates tripled for ages 10-14 and also increased for high school youth (Curtin & Garnett, 2023). Youth suicide rates increased further in the U.S., Japan, and a number of other countries when the covid-19 pandemic began (Bridge et al., 2023; Goto et al., 2022), although increases did not occur in all countries (Lantos & Nyári, 2024).
Along with tragic loss of life, suicide and self-harm have other costs, including billions of dollars in annual medical expenses, reduced quality of life due to injuries, and workforce reductions (Peterson et al., 2024). Furthermore, “suicide contagion” can occur, where knowledge of a suicide or suicide attempt leads to increased suicides, attempts, and/or emotional distress in others (Mueller & Abrutyn, 2015; Shaman et al., 2024). Family members of people who attempt suicide are stressed in multiple ways (Sukmak et al., 2023; Vivekanandhan et al., 2025; Webb et al., 2025). Preventing suicide saves lives and also reduces additional economic harm and spread of mental health struggles to more people.
Prevention in primary and secondary schools, especially universal prevention, may reach more youth and be more equitable than community-based services. Access barriers to mental health care, such as provider shortages, prohibitive financial costs, and stigma, may be less prevalent in schools than community settings (Ayer & Colpe, 2023). For suicide prevention in particular, support that does not rely on parents may be especially important, as research suggests that caregivers are often not aware when a child experiences suicidal ideation (Ayer & Colpe, 2023; DeVille et al., 2020). For the above reasons, some governments have ramped up school-based suicide prevention efforts, such as in the European Union (European Commission, 2017) and Japan (Matsumoto et al., 2024). Educators in the U.S. are also experiencing increased pressure from policymakers and the public to support student mental health in schools (American Foundation for Suicide Prevention, 2024).
When considering school-based suicide prevention interventions, interest-holders need information on what works. Unfortunately, finding accessible, reliable, and specific information on K-12 school-based suicide prevention can be a challenge for education interest-holders. Primary studies often occur in a single geographical location or with a student demographic that may not be relevant for some interest-holders. A handful of systematic reviews with meta-analysis exist to provide a more comprehensive overview of this topic, but their scope and quality varies (see Table 3 and Table 4). Some scientific journal articles are also paywalled or difficult to find. Even when interest-holders have access to the scientific literature, highly technical language and reporting methods used to communicate research results can be difficult to interpret (Baguley, 2009; Belia et al., 2005; Bullock et al., 2019; Gustafson & Rice, 2020).
The goal of this overview of systematic reviews is to provide useful information for interest-holders from a variety of research and non-research backgrounds. We synthesize information from multiple systematic reviews and address issues with risk of bias and review quality.

title: 4  Methods
text:
1 Eligibility Criteria
This overview of systematic reviews was preregistered. The protocol and related materials are available on the Open Science Framework at https://osf.io/4cqsw/overview (Grant, Chinn, et al., 2025).
We included systematic reviews with meta-analysis on effectiveness of K-12 school-based interventions for suicide prevention in students. Our eligibility criteria were based on the PICO framework (Eriksen & Frandsen, 2018) as well as considerations related to timing, setting, publication type, and design (see Table 1). The restriction to English language publications was based on our team’s language abilities and resources. Treatment was defined as intervention after a suicide attempt (e.g., hospitalization) or prevention of a repeat suicide attempt. Postvention was defined as occurring after a suicide with the goal of preventing mental health issues in others. If a review included some eligible and some ineligible populations or settings according to our criteria, such as if a review included both K-12 and university settings, we included it if we determined that it provided information on K-12 school based suicide prevention interventions (e.g., if they conducted a K-12 subgroup analysis or a moderator analysis comparing K-12 to university settings). Grey literature was eligible (e.g., dissertations), but summary-only formats, such as conference abstracts, were not.

title: 4  Methods
text:
2 Search Strategy
We followed the Campbell Collaboration guidance to develop the search strategy (MacDonald et al., 2024; Wilson et al., 2023) and provided a copy of the strategy on the Open Science Framework (https://osf.io/b7dhj/). We conducted a combined search to identify references for both this overview of reviews and our own systematic review. One author (KSF) created the search strategy. Two other authors (SG, LC) reviewed the strategy prior to execution using the Peer Review of Electronic Search Strategies (PRESS) Guideline (McGowan et al., 2016). We applied the PICO framework (Eriksen & Frandsen, 2018) to identify search terms related to each key concept matching our eligibility criteria: (1) school-based, (2) prevention, (3) methods, and (4) suicide. We used a combination of keywords and controlled vocabulary to create search strings specific to each bibliographic database. We employed two text-mining tools, PubMed PubReMiner and Yale MeSH Analyzer, to help identify frequent keywords, MeSH terms, and term variants (Grossetta Nardini & Wang, 2025; Koster, 2014). We ran a scoping search in PubMed and IN SUM (a database of systematic reviews on effects of child and adolescent mental health interventions) to identify potentially eligible publications. We then tested our search strings against the benchmark publications found in the scoping search. After the PRESS review, we added ’pupil*’ keyword to all search strings and wildcards to account for UK spelling in several terms. We did not use published search hedges or database-specific filters. Neither did we apply any date, language, or document format restrictions in our searches. We used DistillerSR ‘Duplicate Detection’ feature to deduplicate our results (DistillerSR Inc., 2025). After our initial search in January 2025, we updated our search monthly in PubMed and quarterly in ProQuest through December 2025.

title: 4  Methods
text:
3 Information Sources
We searched for eligible systematic reviews using the following bibliographic databases (with search platforms in parentheses): PubMed (National Library of Medicine), APA PsycINFO (APA PsycNet), ProQuest Dissertations & Theses Global (ProQuest), and Social Science Premium Collection (ProQuest). The Social Science Premium Collection includes the Criminology Collection, Education Collection (which includes Education Resources Information Center, or “ERIC”), International Bibliography of the Social Sciences, Library & Information Science Collection, Linguistics Collection, Politics Collection, Social Science Database, and Sociology Collection. We also searched the following sources: systematic review and trial registries (IN SUM, clinicaltrials.gov, and WHO International Clinical Trials Registry Platform), web search engines (Google Scholar), regional databases (Australian Education Index), and relevant government and non-government web sites. In addition, we used citationchaser to harvest references from eligible studies and for forward citation chasing. Finally, we hand searched the following journals: Discover Psychology, Mental Health & Prevention, and Revista de Psicodidáctica (English ed.).

title: 4  Methods
text:
4 Selection Process
Citations were uploaded to DistillerSR software, and duplicates were merged using the “Duplicate Detection” feature. All titles and abstracts were screened in DistillerSR according to the eligibility criteria described above. To expedite screening, any report marked as eligible by one screener was retained. When a reference was marked as ineligible by one screener, it was independently screened by a second screener, such that references were only dropped if two screeners marked them ineligible. After screening decisions were made about references identified in the initial January 2025 search, the DistillerSR machine learning tool (DistillerAI) was trained on the team’s screening decisions. This tool assigns a confidence score of 0-1 that represents the likelihood that a reference would be retained by our screeners. We compared the confidence scores to our manual screening decisions from the screening round and identified thresholds to apply the AI tool. For additional search results, we set these thresholds to automatically exclude references with scores ≤ 0.1 and automatically include those with scores ≥ 0.9. References with scores between 0.1 and 0.3 were manually screened by one human team member to verify their exclusion status. References with scores ≥ 0.3 and < 0.9 were screened with the same process as the initial human screening round, where references were only dropped if two human coders marked them as ineligible. This approach follows recommendations to set thresholds based on AI performance on datasets with known decisions, versus applying formal stopping rules (Repke et al., 2026).
Full texts were downloaded for each reference retained during title and abstract screening and were assessed by two authors. Disagreements were resolved by discussion between the two reviewers with input from a third member of the research team as necessary.

title: 4  Methods
text:
5 Data Collection
Data collection, risk of bias, and quality ratings were completed by two independent coders. Discrepancies were resolved via discussion between the two coders, as well as consultation with a third team member when needed. Published updates of reviews were coded independently of initial reviews. Coding was done using forms designed in DistillerSR software. Coders extracted information including bibliographic information (e.g., author name and year), search strategy (e.g., last search date, databases searched), registration number, inclusion/exclusion criteria, sample sizes, and meta-analytic estimates for suicide outcomes. Each eligible systematic review was also given a Risk of Bias in Systematic Reviews rating (ROBIS; Whiting et al., 2016) and a quality rating with A MeaSurement Tool to Assess systematic Reviews–2 (AMSTAR-2; Shea et al., 2017).

title: 4  Methods
text:
6 Synthesis Methods
We narratively report descriptive information for eligible systematic reviews. Next, we summarize overlap across systematic reviews with respect to their included studies. This overlap is illustrated in a citation matrix figure and quantified by Corrected Covered Area (CCA) calculations. Finally, we narratively summarize meta-analytic estimates from eligible systematic reviews with results organized according to PICO (population, intervention, comparison, outcome) categories. Results are discussed in terms of their variation across PICO categorie

title: 5  Results
text:
1 Included Systematic Review Characteristics
Our search yielded 10,547 citations (see Figure 1). After removing duplicates, we screened 8,227 records, retained 1,037 records (12.6%) for full-text eligibility assessment (not all were reviews), and were able to retrieve 1,032 full-texts for eligibility assessment. Of the 1,032 full-texts assessed, we identified 5 eligible systematic reviews with information reported across 6 records (0.5% of full-texts assessed for eligibility; 0% of all citations identified in our search). Appendix 1 lists the reason for exclusion for each full-text assessed. See Appendix 2 for a list of reviews in which we are awaiting to hear more information from the corresponding author to determine eligibility. Appendix 3 contains detailed information on the 5 included systematic reviews.
Reviews varied in their eligibility criteria. Two reviews included studies on effects of child and adolescent school-based prevention on suicide outcomes in children and/or adolescents (Gijzen et al., 2022; Walsh et al., 2022). These reviews included studies that tested effects of suicide-prevention interventions as well as broader intervention targets (e.g., mental health or suicide risk factors like depression) on suicide outcomes (Gijzen et al., 2022; Walsh et al., 2022). Three other reviews focused on prevention-interventions designed specifically for suicide but included older students (Brann et al., 2021; Kiran et al., 2024), did not have age-related eligibility criteria (Pistone et al., 2019), included interventions delivered to school staff (Brann et al., 2021; Kiran et al., 2024), and/or included interventions delivered outside of education settings (e.g., adult gatekeeper training in workplaces; Pistone et al., 2019). . No reviews explicitly excluded screening only programs.
Overall, these 5 reviews were published between 2019 and 2023, with a median publication year of 2022 (see Table 2). These reviews most commonly searched APA PsycInfo (5 reviews; 100%), Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (3 reviews; 60%), MEDLINE (3 reviews; 60%), and ERIC (2 reviews; 40%) to identify primary studies. The year that each review last conducted their literature search ranged from 2017 to 2021, with a median of 2020. Regarding research transparency, 5 reviews (100%) included a flow diagram of information through the different phases of a systematic review (Page et al., 2021), 2 reviews (40%) reported a registration number for their review (Booth et al., 2012), and 2 reviews (40%) provided statements about the data and code underlying the review (Page et al., 2022).

title: 5  Results
text:
2 Overlap Across Systematic Reviews
The number of primary studies included in each review ranged from 12 to 49, with a median of 25. Of the 78 primary studies included across all reviews, 36 studies (46.2%) were included in more than one review (see Figure 2). The overall CCA percentage for all primary studies included across reviews was 19.9%, indicating high overlap in primary studies across reviews. No single review identified all primary studies on school-based suicide prevention interventions.

title: 5  Results
section: 3 Critical Appraisal of Included Systematic Reviews
text:
3 Critical Appraisal of Included Systematic Reviews
Appendix 4 contains our critical appraisal assessments (i.e., ratings and rationale) for each item in both instruments across all included reviews. 
AMSTAR-2 assessments (see Table 3) yielded methodological quality ratings of low for 1 reviews (20%) and critically low for 4 reviews (80%). The most common critical weaknesses in methodological quality were not providing justification for excluding individual studies (5 reviews; 100%), not considering risk of bias when interpreting the results of the review (3 reviews; 60%), and not having a protocol registered before commencement of the review (3 reviews; 60%). The most common non-critical weaknesses in methodological quality were not reporting funding sources for included studies (5 reviews; 100%), not assessing the impact of risk of bias in individual studies on evidence synthesis results (4 reviews; 80%), and not explaining the selection criteria used to identify included study designs (3 reviews; 60%).
ROBIS assessments (see Table 4) yielded ratings of low risk of bias for 1 reviews (20%, unclear risk of bias for 1 review 20%), and high risk of bias for 3 reviews (60%). Concerns about risk of bias were most commonly related to study eligibility criteria (4 reviews; 80%), identification and selection of studies (4 reviews; 80%), and synthesis and findings (4 reviews; 80%).

title: 5  Results
text:
4 Review Findings
We identified meta-analytic estimates of suicide outcomes for school-based prevention interventions overall (Table 4), as well as for specific intervention approaches or contrasts (Table 5). Two reviews (Kiran et al. 2023 and Pistone et al. 2019) only reported moderator analyses but did not report overall effects of school-based suicide prevention interventions; consequently, they do not have results reported in Table 4 and are reported in Table 5.

4.1 Overall Effects
Meta-analytic estimates reported here were derived mainly from primary studies described as meeting our inclusion/exclusion criteria. However, some analyses may have included a small number of primary studies not meeting our inclusion/exclusion criteria. Specifically, the Gijzen (2022) and Brann (2021) meta-analyses included a study with gatekeeper and screening intervention arms (Wasserman et al., 2015), but most studies in the analyses were reported as containing interventions delivered to students. Walsh et al. (2022) included a small number of screening or gatekeeper programs in their systematic review, but it is unclear if any or how many of these were included in the meta-analyses reported in this section.

4.1.1 Suicidal behaviors
Gijzen and colleagues (2022) found that school-based prevention interventions were associated with fewer suicidal behaviors at 0-3 months post-test relative to no intervention, placebo, and usual care control groups (SMD = .30, 95% CI .15 to .45, p <.001). Heterogeneity was high (I2 = 94, 95% CI 90 to 96). The same review also found benefits at 3-12 months (SMD = 0.30, 95% CI 0.16 to 0.43, p <.001). Walsh and colleagues (2022) also found that interventions were associated with fewer suicide attempts relative to comparison groups at 0- to 20-month follow-ups (adjusted log OR = .72, 95% CI .59 to .87). This analysis had no significant heterogeneity (Q18 = 10.46, p = .49; within-study heterogeneity = 9.10%, between-study heterogeneity = 0%). Brann (2021) found a small effect indicating fewer suicidal behaviors for intervention relative to control groups in high school (SMD = .17, 95% CI .06 to .26, p < .01).


4.1.2 Suicidal ideation outcomes
Gijzen and colleagues (2022) found a small benefit of school-based prevention interventions on suicidal ideation outcomes relative to no intervention, placebo, and usual care control groups at 0-3 months post-test (SMD = .15, 95% CI .06 to .24, p = .001, k = 10 effect sizes). Heterogeneity was high (I2 = 85, 95% CI 73 to 91). Walsh and colleagues (2022) examined all follow-up time points (0-20 months) and results suggested that school-based prevention interventions reduced suicidal ideation in adolescents relative to no intervention, other intervention, and waitlist control comparisons (adjusted log OR = .85, 95% CI .75 to .95). This analysis did not have significant heterogeneity (Q18 = 10.04, p = .93, within-study and between-study heterogeneity = 0.0%).



4.2 Effects of Intervention Approach

4.2.1 Targeting versus not targeting STBs
Two systematic reviews tested the effect of whether interventions primarily targeted suicidal thoughts and behaviors (STBs) versus interventions not designed specifically for STBs, such as those targeting risk factors for suicide or general mental health. Gijzen and colleagues (2022) found mixed results. For suicidal behaviors, they found that interventions primarily targeting STBs were less effective compared to those targeting risk factors (b = − 0.87, SEb = 0.28, z = − 3.04, 95% CI − 1.42 to − 0.31, p = .002), but those targeting STBs were still found to be beneficial (SMD = .23, p < .001). For suicidal ideation outcomes, a meta-regression indicated that interventions primarily targeting STBs did not produce significantly different effects than those targeting risk-factors (b = − 0.22, SEb = 0.15, z = − 1.49, 95% CI − 0.50 to − 0.07, p = .136). These results should be interpreted with caution due to inclusion of only one primary study that did not target STBs and high heterogeneity. Walsh and colleagues (2022) also conducted meta-regressions on effects of interventions primarily targeting STBs. They found that interventions primarily targeting STBs were associated with fewer suicide attempts relative to controls (adjusted log OR = .67, p <.001), but those not primarily targeting STBs showed no statistically significant benefits (adjusted log OR = 1.11; adjusted log OR for contrast = -.51 (95% CI -1.04 to .02)).


4.2.2 Educational interventions
Pistone and colleagues (2019) focused on educational interventions, described as designed to help identify people at risk of suicide. In an analysis excluding gatekeeper trainings, they found that school-based education interventions were associated with fewer suicide attempts relative to comparison groups at 3-month follow up (OR = .56, 95% CI .39 to .80, p = .001, k = 3 studies) and 12-month follow-up (OR = .60, 95% CI .38 to .95, p = .03, k = 2 studies). A meta-analysis on suicidal ideation found no statistically significant benefit at 3-month follow-up (OR = .82, 95% CI .56 to 1.19, p = .29, k = 2 studies), but there was an observed benefit at 12-month follow-up (OR = .54, 95% CI .35 to .81, p = .003, k = 2 studies).



4.3 Effect of School Level for Universal Prevention
Kiran and colleagues (2023) conducted a meta-regression comparing effects of school (middle-high school) versus college-level universal prevention targeting pupils/students on suicidality outcomes. Results indicated that intervention effectiveness did not significantly differ by school level (B = 0.178, 95% CI -1.116 to 1.471, p = .788).


4.4 Effect of Follow-Up Timing
Walsh and colleagues (2022) performed contrasts comparing suicide attempt outcomes at ≤ 3 months to 12- and 17-to 20-month follow-ups. Interventions groups had fewer suicide attempts than comparison groups at ≤ 3 months (adjusted log OR = .70, p < .01, k = 6 effects from 4 studies) and 12 months (adjusted log OR = .59, p < .01, k = 3 effects from 1 study), but not at 17 to 20 months (adjusted log OR = 1.06, k = 3 effects from 3 studies). However, neither contrast was statistically significant (12 months vs. ≤ 3 months log OR = -.16, 95% CI -.59 to .26; 17-20 months vs. ≤ 3 months log OR =.42, 95% CI -.11 to .95). As noted above, this review included some interventions not primarily targeting STBs, such as those targeting STB risk factors.

title: 6  Discussion
text:
1 Implications for Policy and Practice
We identified 5 eligible systematic reviews that included 78 primary studies across all reviews. Researchers conceptualized suicide prevention in multiple ways. For instance, some interventions aim to prevent suicide via risk factors such as depression or mental health, while others directly targeted STBs. Overall, results generally suggest that school-based suicide prevention programs have modest positive average effects on suicide-related outcomes. Two systematic reviews with meta-analyses of randomized trials found that school-based prevention interventions reduced both suicidal ideation and suicide attempts/behaviors relative to controls overall at short-term and longer-term follow-ups (Gijzen et al., 2022; Walsh et al., 2022). In addition, findings suggest several factors that may impact effectiveness. In terms of intervention approach, interventions primarily targeting STBs may reduce suicide attempts (Walsh et al., 2022). For suicidal behaviors more broadly, interventions targeting risk factors may produce larger effects than those primarily targeting STBs (Gijzen et al., 2022). Educational interventions designed to identify people at risk of suicide may yield fewer suicide attempts at both 3-month and 12-month follow-ups, with mixed evidence for effects on suicidal ideation (Pistone et al., 2019). In terms of student age and school level, one review did not detect differences between effects at the middle/high school level versus the college level for universal prevention targeting suicidality outcomes (Kiran et al., 2024). One review conducted an analysis specifically on high school populations and also detected a small benefit on suicidal behaviors (Brann et al., 2021). In terms of follow-up timing, evidence suggests fewer suicide attempts among intervention groups relative to comparison groups at shorter (≤ 3 months) and medium-term (12-month) follow-ups, but not at longer-term (17–20 month) follow-ups (Walsh et al., 2022). Given limitations in the current body of primary evidence on these interventions, future research is needed to improve confidence in these moderating factors.

title: 6  Discussion
text: Taken together, the results summarized in this overview indicate that schools may be a promising setting for suicide prevention. Effect sizes across existing systematic reviews are small, but small effect sizes are common in youth mental health prevention research (Tanner-Smith et al., 2018). Since suicidality is a top contributor to youth mortality in many countries, the number of students who would potentially benefit from even modestly effective suicide prevention interventions in schools is substantial. Still, the evidence is uncertain, given some inconsistent results, low AMSTAR-2 quality ratings, high ROBIS risk of bias ratings, and author concerns about publication bias and quality of primary studies. Therefore, we suggest that interest-holders weigh potential benefits against practical factors such as resource constraints. When considering specific programs, interest-holders should also consider how well programs would fit in their local contexts (E. Day et al., 2025).

title: 6  Discussion
text:
2 Implications for Future Research
Our assessments of review quality and meta-analytic risk of bias suggest a new systematic review may be warranted to provide users of this evidence base with an up-to-date synthesis of the literature and to overcome methodological limitations of existing reviews. For instance, this review could address reasons for lower AMSTAR-2 and ROBIS ratings in most reviews, such as a prospectively registered protocol and analysis plan, a comprehensive search strategy (including gray literature and studies not published in English), incorporation of satisfactory tools for risk of bias assessment into syntheses, and use of formal approaches to rating and communicating certainty of evidence (Guyatt et al., 2008; Santesso et al., 2020). In addition, this systematic review could provide estimates of direct evidence: that is, suicide-focused prevention interventions (as current reviews often covered interventions not focused on suicide) and interventions delivered during the school day (as current reviews often combined evidence for interventions delivered during the school day with those delivered on school premises but outside of normal school hours). For example, some reviews included interventions not delivered directly to students, such as gatekeeper interventions for school staff, and review eligibility criteria did not usually specify intervention recipients. To address many of these needs, we plan to conduct a new, standalone, living systematic review on school-based suicide prevention interventions delivered to students during normal school hours, as part of a meta-analytic research domain (Cuijpers et al., 2022) on school-based mental health prevention programs.

title: 6  Discussion
text:
3 Limitations and Future Directions
While this overview has several strengths, we note several limitations. First, our search may not have identified all eligible reviews. While we did not use language restrictions in our search strategy, we did not have proficiency in languages other than English on our review team. We also did not contact content experts and organizations for missing reviews nor search systematic review-specific repositories (e.g., Epistemonikos, KSR Evidence, PROSPERO, Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews) for completed or ongoing reviews. Future updates to this overview can incorporate non-English publications, contact experts and organizations, and search review registries. Second, we did not contact systematic review authors to validate our methodological analysis or obtain missing information (Faggion et al., 2023). We also did not reassess reported information, reanalyze data to conduct our own meta-analyses and sensitivity analyses, or independently obtain desired information when missing. Third, we only collected information on the effects of interventions on suicide-related outcomes. Some reviews reported additional outcomes not originally in our protocol, such as improvements in suicide awareness and helping skills associated with intervention. Other types of evidence critical to the delivery of school-based suicide prevention interventions include equity, feasibility of implementation, acceptability to key interest-holders, intervention costs, cultural considerations, and adverse effects such as contagion and increased distress following exposure to suicide-related content (Abrutyn & Mueller, 2014; Shaman et al., 2024). Finally, we did not include supplemental primary studies in this overview, limiting the evidence in this article to that synthesized and reported in existing systematic reviews. We also did not examine and manage discrepant data (i.e., data from the same primary study reported differently across eligible systematic reviews) during data collection and analysis. Outcome definitions varied across the included reviews in ways we did not harmonize, and differences in measurement instruments, time frames, and rater types may contribute to heterogeneity in reported estimates and limit direct comparability across reviews. Our citation matrix was constructed at the primary study level and did not account for whether different reviews meta-analyzed the same or different effect sizes from within a primary study, which could lead to an overestimate of overlap relative to overlap at the meta-analytic level (Bracchiglione et al., 2026).

title: 6  Discussion
text:
4 Conclusions
The goal of this overview was to synthesize systematic reviews with meta-analyses focused on the effects of suicide prevention interventions for primary and secondary students in school settings. School-based suicide prevention programs may yield modest yet possibly meaningful reductions in suicidal behaviors and ideation among primary and secondary school students on average. However, limitations in both the primary evidence and existing systematic reviews caution against conclusive recommendations, such as policies mandating suicide prevention interventions in schools. High-quality primary and secondary research is still needed to better understand the effects of suicide prevention interventions in schools.

title: 7  References
text:

NR
Walsh 2022



Note. NR = not reported, SMD = standardized mean difference, CI = confidence interval, STBs = suicidal thoughts and behaviors, OR = odds ratio.

title: 10  Additional Information
text:
2 Funding
Core funding for the HEDCO Institute for Evidence-Based Educational Practice is provided by donor funds to the University of Oregon. The funders had no role in study design, data collection and analysis, preparation of this manuscript, or decisions about publication and other dissemination.

title: 10  Additional Information
text:
3 Data Availablility
The study registration, including the protocol and analysis plan, data, and code for this overview of systematic reviews is publicly available on the Open Science Framework at https://osf.io/4cqsw/overview.

SG’s spouse is an employee of Eli Lilly and owns stock. All other authors have no disclosures to report.

3 Appendix 3. Descriptive Characteristics for Included Systematic Reviews



  Appendix 3
  Characteristics of Included Reviews


  
  













Review
Brann 2021


Title
A meta-analysis of suicide prevention programs for school-aged youth


Main Focus


Suicide: yes
Prevention: yes
School-aged youth: yes
School settings: yes




Eligibility Criteria


Participants: Inclusion: targeted youth populations, which included K-12 students or college students
Interventions: Inclusion: targeting suicide through prevention/intervention or outcome measure, included a treatment group with a suicide prevention focus.
Comparisons: Inclusion: included a control group
Outcomes: Include: targeting suicide through prevention/intervention or outcome measure, included sufficient treatment and control group data to calculate effect sizes post intervention
Timing: Not reported
Setting: Inclusion: school or college setting
Design: Include: RCTs, cluster-randomized trials, and quasi-experimental designs




Search Strategy


Databases searched: Education Research Complete, ERIC, PSYCArticles, PsycINFO, Psychology and BehavioralSciences,first 600 dissertation results on ProQuest
Search date: 2019
Included studies: 25




Methodological Transparency


PRISMA diagram: Yes
Registration: Not reported
Data availability statement: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.childyouth.2020.105826
Conflict of interest: The authors declared no competing interests




Critical Appraisal


AMSTAR-2: Critically low
ROBIS: High




References
Brann, Kristy L, Baker, Derek, Smith-Millman, Mills K, Watt, Sarah J, DiOrio, Courtney (2021/02). A meta-analysis of suicide prevention programs for school-aged youth Children and Youth Services Review, 121, 1. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.childyouth.2020.105826




 
  
  













Review
Gijzen 2022


Title
Effectiveness of school-based preventive programs in suicidal thoughts and behaviors: A meta-analysis


Main Focus


Suicide: yes
Prevention: yes
School-aged youth: yes
School settings: yes




Eligibility Criteria


Participants: Include: the participants were children or adolescents up to the age of 25, but studies did not only include participants older than 18 years. (Note that the studies they actually found only went up to HS at the oldest.)
Interventions: The authors do not list intervention criteria in the inclusion/exclusion criteria section. However, the intro states "the current review and meta-analysis will not be restricted to studies that aim to reduce suicidality, but will include all studies that have included STBs as an outcome measurement to explore potential effects of studies that target risk factors of STBs. As such, the current meta-analyses aims to review the literature to identify and analyze effectiveness of school-based preventive strategies on STBs."
Comparisons: Include: control group who received either no intervention, placebo, or usual care. Note that the exclusion criteria do not mention studies without a control group, but the results mention excluding studies without a control group.
Outcomes: Include: the outcome of the study included a measure of suicidal thoughts and behaviors. The results mention excluding/not analyzing studies that do not look at suicidal thoughts and behaviors, such as ones that only looked at attitudes toward suicide or help-seeking intentions.
Timing: Not reported
Setting: Include: school setting
Design: Include: assignment of individuals to the intervention and control groups in included studies was random (i.e. conducted as a randomized controlled trial); published in the English or Dutch language; the data are reported such that effect sizes could be computed; studies were using original data. Note that the exclusion criteria do not mention non-randomized designs, but the results mention excluding non-randomized studies.




Search Strategy


Databases searched: Medline, PsycInfo, Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials, EMBASE
Search date: Not reported
Included studies: 12




Methodological Transparency


PRISMA diagram: Yes
Registration: Not reported
Data availability statement: Not reported
Conflict of interest: The authors declared no competing interests




Critical Appraisal


AMSTAR-2: Critically low
ROBIS: Unclear




References
Gijzen, M., W., M., Rasing, S., P., A., Creemers, D., H., M., Engels, R., C., M., E., Smit, F. (2022). Effectiveness of school-based preventive programs in suicidal thoughts and behaviors: A meta-analysis Journal of Affective Disorders, Part A. 298, 408. 10.1016/j.jad.2021.10.062




 
  
  













Review
Kiran 2024


Title
Controlled interventions to improve suicide prevention in educational settings: A systematic review and network meta-analysis


Main Focus


Suicide: yes
Prevention: yes
School-aged youth: yes
School settings: yes




Eligibility Criteria


Participants: Included studies involved children and young people aged between 12 and 25 years from any sociodemographic background, or staff members in educational settings working with young people aged between 12 and 25 years.
Interventions: Included: universal interventions (i.e., targeting the entire population of youngpeople or staff members at educational settings), selective interventions (i.e., targeting young people at high risk or educational or health care staff members at educational settings dealing with young people at risk) and indicated interventions (i.e., targeting young people already exhibiting high risk for suicide). Key intervention contents included education and/or awareness of suicide risk in young people, gatekeeper suicide prevention training of educational staff that involves the identification and referral of the at-risk young people (Cross et al., 2011), improved screening of young people for suicide risk by health care professionals, and psychotherapies
Comparisons: Include: Contained comparator groups such as no treatment, waiting list control, minimal intervention such as information provision and referral to community services (e.g., healthcare centers), usual care (UC), or fully active interventions.
Outcomes: Include: primary outcome data on STBs (e.g., suicide attempts, suicidal behaviors,or suicidal ideation) at postintervention or suicide prevention competencies (suicide-related knowledge, help-seeking skills, and attitudes) at postintervention. Other secondary outcomes included depression, anxiety, and hopelessness
Timing: Not reported
Setting: Include: Were based at schools, colleges, and universities of any country.
Design: Include: Employed controlled intervention research designs including randomized controlled trials (RCTs), cluster RCTs, crossover trials, controlled before–after studies, and quasi-experimental study designs.




Search Strategy


Databases searched: Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (CENTRAL), Medline, PsycInfo, ClinicalTrials.gov, OpenTrials.net
Search date: April 2021
Included studies: 49




Methodological Transparency


PRISMA diagram: Yes
Registration: CRD42020208723
Data availability statement: Data are readily available upon request.
Conflict of interest: The authors declared no competing interests and/or disclosed funding sources and management of any potential conflicts




Critical Appraisal


AMSTAR-2: Low
ROBIS: Low




References
Kiran, T., Angelakis, I., Panagioti, M., Irshad, S., Sattar, R., Hidayatullah, S., Tyler, N., Tofique, S., Bukhsh, A., Eylem-van, Bergeijk, O., Ozen-Dursun, B., Husain, N., Chaudhry, N., Hodkinson, A. (2023). Controlled interventions to improve suicide prevention in educational settings: A systematic review and network meta-analysis Clinical Psychology: Science and Practice, No. 10.1037/cps0000179




 
  
  













Review
Pistone 2019


Title
The effects of educational interventions on suicide: A systematic review and meta-analysis


Main Focus


Suicide: yes
Prevention: yes
School-aged youth: yes
School settings: yes




Eligibility Criteria


Participants: No participant inclusion/exclusion criteria noted in selection criteria section of manuscript, but PRISMA chart does indicate exclusions based on population.
Interventions: Include: education interventions. Exclude: studies evaluating promotion, treatment, and maintenance interventions
Comparisons: Not reported
Outcomes: Include: suicidal behaviour (suicide, attempts and ideation) and intermediate outcomes (knowledge, attitudes and skills)
Timing: Not reported
Setting: Exclude: interventions developed for and conducted in the specific context of prisons, in health care or the national defence forces
Design: Include: studies using a randomised controlled trial (RCT) or quasi-experimental design, Exclude: Results section indicates exclusions were made based on high risk of bias. PRISMA chart indicates some excluded due to language and publication type.




Search Strategy


Databases searched: Scopus, PubMed, PsycINFO, ASSIA
Search date: May 07, 2017
Included studies: 41




Methodological Transparency


PRISMA diagram: Yes
Registration: Not reported
Data availability statement: Not reported
Conflict of interest: Lacks reporting on funding and/or conflicts of interest




Critical Appraisal


AMSTAR-2: Critically low
ROBIS: High




References
Pistone, I., Beckman, U., Eriksson, E., Lagerlof, H., Sager, M. (2019). The effects of educational interventions on suicide: A systematic review and meta-analysis International Journal of Social Psychiatry, 20764019852655. 10.1177/0020764019852655




 
  
  













Review
Walsh 2022


Title
Research Review: The effect of school-based suicide prevention on suicidal ideation and suicide attempts and the role of intervention and contextual factors among adolescents: a meta-analysis and meta-regression


Main Focus


Suicide: yes
Prevention: yes
School-aged youth: yes
School settings: yes




Eligibility Criteria


Participants: Include: adolescents aged 11–19 years attending post-primary school
Interventions: Intervention criteria described in terms of settings (indicated under Settings), not really characteristics of the intervention
Comparisons: Include: contained comparators including no intervention, other intervention, and wait-list control
Outcomes: Include: measuring suicidal thoughts and behaviors (STBs), including suicidal ideation, suicide attempts, planning, and death by suicide.
Timing: Not reported
Setting: Include: interventions conducted in post-primary school settings
Design: Include: employed CRT study designs. Exclude: non-CRT studies




Search Strategy


Databases searched: PsycINFO, Medline, Education Source, ERIC, Web of Science, Cochrane Central RegisterofControlled Trials
Search date: January 2021
Included studies: 13




Methodological Transparency


PRISMA diagram: Yes
Registration: CRD42020168883
Data availability statement: Not reported
Conflict of interest: The authors declared no competing interests




Critical Appraisal


AMSTAR-2: Critically low
ROBIS: High




References
Walsh, E., H., McMahon, J., Herring, M., P. (2022). Research Review: The effect of school-based suicide prevention on suicidal ideation and suicide attempts and the role of intervention and contextual factors among adolescents: a meta-analysis and meta-regression Journal of Child Psychology & Psychiatry & Allied Disciplines, 15, 15. 10.1111/jcpp.13598

Walsh, Eibhlin (2023). Rethinking post-primary school-based suicide prevention: understanding intrvention effectiveness, implementation requirements and youth voice.
